coredata.dc:title: Green synthesized carbon quantum dots as chemiluminescence sensor for sulfanilamide detection
coredata.dc:creator: Zhao, Yue
Yang, Xian
Han, Hongping
Zhang, Luoyilan
Cheng, Ke
Xiao, Yujie
Tan, Xuan
Xue, Hao
Zhang, Xue
Zheng, Hong
coredata.dc:description: N–S co-doped quantum carbon dots (N–S-CQD) were synthesized using a hydrothermal method with Artemisia annua L. as a precursor and dl-cysteine as the nitrogen-sulfur source. N–S-CQD can enhance the chemiluminescence (CL) intensity of KMnO4 system, while sulfanilamide (SA) is capable of significantly inhibit the CL intensity of KMnO4-(N–S-CQD) system. Based on this finding, we developed a novel CL method for the determination of SA. Under the optimal conditions, SA could be accurately determined in the concentration range of 50–1600 μM with a limit of detection of 0.22 μM. The investigation of potential interferences and recovery rates confirmed the method's suitability for assessing biomass samples.
coredata.dcterms:subject: Artemisia annua L.
Carbon quantum dots
Sulfanilamide
N–S co-doped
Chemiluminescence

xocs:item-toc-entry.xocs:item-toc-section-title: CL signals of four CQD and the effect of N–S-CQD on the KMnO4 system
Optical properties and CL stability of N–S-CQD
The linear response range and limit of detection

xocs:funding-list.xocs:funding-text: We gratefully acknowledge the Science and Technology Research Program of the Chongqing Municipal Education Commission (No. KJZD-K202300504 ).

ce:caption.ce:simple-para: (a) CL signals of the four CQD in the KMnO4 system, the insets are pictures of N–S-CQD, S-CQD, N-CQD and CQD in natural light, respectively; (b) CL signals of the KMnO4-(N–S-CQD) system and the KMnO4 system.
(a–c) TEM images of N–S-CQD at different magnifications, the insets of (a) and (c) are histogram of the size distribution of N–S-CQD and lattice streak images.
(a) Full XPS spectra of N–S-CQD and CQD; (b–e) Spectra of C 1s, O 1s, N 1s and S 2p of N–S-CQD; (f–h) Spectra of C 1s, O 1s and N 1s of CQD.
(a–c) FTIR spectra, XRD spectra and Raman spectra of N–S-CQD and CQD.

(a) UV spectra, FL excitation and emission spectra of N–S-CQD, the insets are N–S-CQD in visible light (left) and 365 nm UV light (right); (b) FL emission spectra of N–S-CQD at different excitation wavelengths (Ex = 300–380 nm); (c) The CL intensity of N–S-CQD after different storage times, the insets are the state of N–S-CQD storage for different times.
(a–d) The effect of N–S-CQD concentration, HCl concentration, HCHO concentration and KMnO4 concentration on CL intensity; (e) linear regression equation of SA; (f) CL kinetic curve.
Effect of common interfering substances on SA determination.

row.entry: Mg2+, H2PO4 −, HPO4 2−, PO4 3−, Anhydrous glucose
Zn2+, HCOO−, Lys, Arg, Gly, Citric acid

ce:caption.ce:simple-para: Recovery of SA in pork, chicken and fish samples.
The results of SA determination using different analytical methods.

row.entry: Lake water, Synthetic saliva, sweat and urine

head.ce:title: Green synthesized carbon quantum dots as chemiluminescence sensor for sulfanilamide detection

ce:affiliation.ce:label: a
ce:affiliation.ce:textfn: Engineering Research Center of Active Substance Biotechnology, Ministry of Education, College of Life Sciences, Chongqing Normal University, Chongqing, 401331, China

sa:affiliation.sa:organization: Engineering Research Center of Active Substance Biotechnology

ce:affiliation.ce:textfn: College of Pharmacy, Chongqing Medical and Pharmaceutical College, Chongqing, 401331, China

ce:affiliation.ce:textfn: College of Chemistry and Chemical Engineering, Qinghai Normal University, Qinghai, 810000, China

sa:affiliation.sa:organization: College of Chemistry and Chemical Engineering

ce:affiliation.ce:source-text: College of Chemistry and Chemical Engineering, Qinghai Normal University, Qinghai 810000, China

ce:affiliation.ce:textfn: Central Lab of Chongqing Water Conservancy Investment Group Co., LTD, Chongqing, 401331, China

sa:affiliation.sa:organization: Central Lab of Chongqing Water Conservancy Investment Group Co., LTD

ce:affiliation.ce:source-text: Central Laboratory of Chongqing Water Conservancy Investment (Group) Company Limited, Chongqing 401100, China

ce:abstract-sec.ce:simple-para: N–S co-doped quantum carbon dots (N–S-CQD) were synthesized using a hydrothermal method with Artemisia annua L. as a precursor and dl-cysteine as the nitrogen-sulfur source. N–S-CQD can enhance the chemiluminescence (CL) intensity of KMnO4 system, while sulfanilamide (SA) is capable of significantly inhibit the CL intensity of KMnO4-(N–S-CQD) system. Based on this finding, we developed a novel CL method for the determination of SA. Under the optimal conditions, SA could be accurately determined in the concentration range of 50–1600 μM with a limit of detection of 0.22 μM. The investigation of potential interferences and recovery rates confirmed the method's suitability for assessing biomass samples.

A new type of N–S co-doped carbon dots (N–S-CQD) were synthesized using one-pot hydrothermal method with Artemisia annua L. as the carbon source and dl-cysteine as the co-dopant, further investigation suggested that N–S-CQD could be applied to the detection of sulfanilamide.
Image 1

ce:data-availability.ce:para: No data was used for the research described in the article.

ce:section.ce:para: Sulfanilamide (SA) is an economy-effective medication with broad-spectrum antimicrobial properties, it's commonly used to prevent and treat bacterial infections in livestock and poultry [1]. However, misuse of SA can cause allergic reactions, hematopoietic disorders and even organ damage [2]. Residues of SA tend to accumulate in livestock and poultry products due to their limited biodegradability. Consequently, it is utmost importance to explore effective methods for SA detection [3]. Several methods have been developed to determine SA, including high performance liquid chromatography (HPLC) [4], mass spectrometry (MS) [5], capillary electrophoresis (CE) [6] and spectrophotometric detection (SD) [7]. While these methods offer high precision, they do come with certain limitations, such as restricted detection ranges, suboptimal reproducibility and difficulty in trace detection. Thus, the imperative lies to devise a detection method for SA detection that combines simplicity, efficacy, environmental friendliness and cost-efficiency.

Chemiluminescence (CL) is an analytical and detection method that relies on the intensity of luminescence produced by chemical reactions. CL offers several advantages, including simple instrumentation, rapid detection, low cost and high sensitivity [8]. This method has found numerous applications in various fields, encompassing bio-imaging [9], immunoassays [10] and sensor technologies [11]. Consequently, CL has emerged as a highly efficient analytical technique with substantial potential for the detection of SA.

Carbon quantum dots (CQD) are a type of carbon-based nano-materials that are typically less than 10 nm in size, which have numerous advantages, including exceptional luminescent properties, ease of preparation and low toxicity [12]. These properties have led to a wide range of applications in medical diagnostics [13], composition detection [14], material science [15] and new energy [16]. The synthesis of CQD can be achieved through various methods, with options including electrochemical synthesis [17], solvothermal synthesis [18], calcination synthesis [19] and microwave assisted synthesis [20]. Among the many synthetic precursors, natural products are a reliable source for the synthesis of CQD, being readily available, cost-effective and renewable [21]. Many natural products have proven effective in the synthesis of carbon dots, resulting in notable achievements in practical applications. For instance, CQD synthesized from green tea can function as natural antioxidants and antibacterial agents [22], the synthesis of CQD using waste sunflower straw serve as a fluorescent probe to detect Cu2+ [23]. In addition, the luminescent properties of CQD can be improved by doping with atoms such as N, P and S [24,25].

Artemisia annua L. serves as a natural source of the potent anti-malarial drug artemisinin which has a long history of use as a medicinal herb in China [26]. It contains several bioactive constituents, including sesquiterpene, diterpene, flavonoids, coumarin, polysaccharides and 3-Phenylpropanoic acid [27]. These active ingredients can contribute to significant pharmaceutical effects and chemical reactions. Artemisia annua L. is an ideal precursor material for the synthesis of CQD because it is widely available, easily accessible, cost-effective and environmentally friendly.

In this study, N–S-CQD were efficiently prepared by hydrothermal method using Artemisia annua L. as the green precursor and dl-cysteine as a co-dopant for nitrogen and sulfur. The N–S-CQD have small particle sizes, uniform distribution, good water solubility and outstanding stable CL properties, which enhanced the CL signal of KMnO4 system. A new system of KMnO4-(N–S-CQD) was established, the CL intensity of the new system is inhibited by SA and the inhibitory effect is proportional to the concentration of SA. Thus, an effective CL method was built for the determination of SA in real samples, yielding satisfactory results.

Artemisia annua L. was collected on the campus of Chongqing Normal University (Chongqing, China). Sulfanilamide (Tianjin Guangfu Fine Chemical Research Institute, China). dl-cysteine (Shanghai Macklin Biochemical Co., Ltd, China). Potassium permanganate (Chengdu Kelong Chemical Co., Ltd, China). Hydrochloric acid, formaldehyde, ethyl acetate and hexane (Aladdin Chemical Co., Ltd., China) The pork, chicken and fish samples were purchased from the local market (Chongqing, China). All chemical reagents are analytical purity.

The morphology and structure of N–S-CQD on the Cu network were scanned using a Tecnai G2 F30 field emission transmission electron microscope (TEM, FEI, USA) at 300 KV. The elements C, O, N and S were scanned by ESCALAB 250Xi X-ray photoelectron spectroscopy (XPS, Thermo Fisher, USA) and the binding energy of C1 was adjusted to 284.8 eV to correct for charge effects. Fourier transform infrared spectra (FTIR) were obtained using a Nicolet iS10 FTIR spectrometer (Thermo Fisher, USA) to identify different types of functional groups in the spectral range of 4000-400 cm−1. X-ray diffraction (XRD) was obtained using a D8 ADVANCE X-ray diffractometer (Bruker, GER) with a diffraction range of 10°–80° and a diffraction rate of 5°/minute. Raman spectra (Raman) in the range of 100–3500 cm−1 were collected using an inVia Raman spectrometer (Renishaw, UK) with an excitation wavelength λ = 532 nm and measured three times in parallel. CL spectra (IFFM-D, Remex, CHN) were obtained at a high pressure of 700 V and a pump speed of 30 r/min. UV–vis absorption spectra (UV) at 200–800 nm were recorded with a TU-1810DSPC UV spectrophotometer (Persee, China). Fluorescence spectra (FL) were obtained by fluorescence spectrophotometer (F-7100, Hitachi, JPN) under a 150 W Xe lamp.

Dry and grind clean dried Artemisia annua L. into a powder, add 50 ml deionized water to 1 g Artemisia annua L. powder and sonicate for 40 min, then placed in a hydrothermal synthesis reactor at 190 °C for 4 h, name the obtained product as CQD. Furthermore, 1.0 mL of ammonia, 1.0 mL concentrated sulfuric acid and 0.1 g dl-cysteine were added to the sonicated solution of Artemisia annua L., respectively, while keeping all other conditions constant, the products were named N-CQD, S-CQD and N–S-CQD, respectively. The obtained product was filtered several times through a 0.22 μm membrane to remove the residue. Finally, it was dialyzed through 500–1000 Da dialysis membranes for 2 days to obtain solutions of the four types of CQD.

SA was detected using a CL system composed of HCl, KMnO4, HCHO and N–S-CQD. The procedure for determining SA is shown in Fig. 1 . HCl, KMnO4 and HCHO solutions are pumped in by pump 1, respectively, while the N–S-CQD is pumped in by pump 2. At the confluence of the fluid mixer, each solution undergoes a redox reaction, resulting in a stable baseline CL signal, noted as I. When the inlet valve is switched, the SA solution is introduced into the flow path through pump 2, that initiate the sample detection and generate a sample CL signal, noted as I0. The relative CL intensity is calculated as the peak height (ΔI = I – I
0 ). To achieve the optimal experimental conditions for detecting SA, the concentrations of HCl, KMnO4, HCHO and N–S-CQD were optimized. The detection range, detection limit and precision of SA were determined under optimal conditions and the luminescence mechanism of the system was investigated.

ce:section.ce:section-title: CL signals of four CQD and the effect of N–S-CQD on the KMnO4 system
ce:section.ce:para: Fig. 2 (a) displays the CL signals of four different types of CQD in KMnO4 system. N–S-CQD exhibit the strongest CL signals, followed by S-CQD and N-CQD with CQD showing the weakest CL signal. The significant enhancement of CL signals in N–S-CQD is likely due to the more effective modification of the material structure by N–S co-doped, thus amplifying the CL signals of N–S-CQD, indicating modifying surface groups can result in the creation of novel carbon nanoparticles with exceptional CL properties.

Based on the data presented in Fig. 2(b), the KMnO4 system has a low CL intensity, whereas the CL signal of the new system formed by the addition of N–S-CQD is significantly enhanced, which is attributed to the fact that N–S-CQD contains functional groups such as –NH2, –OH and –COOH, which can enhance the oxidative properties of KMnO4 and accelerate the redox reaction, ultimately affecting the intensity of CL.

The size distribution and morphological characteristics of the materials were evaluated using TEM and HRTEM images. Fig. 3 displays the morphology of N–S-CQD at different magnifications. As observed in Fig. 3(a–b), the N–S-CQD is spheroidal, uniform in size and good dispersion. A histogram of the diameter distribution of N–S-CQD was produced by measuring approximately 1600 particles (inset in Fig. 3(a)). From the statistical results, it can be seen that the diameter distribution of N–S-CQD ranges from 0.90 to 5.97 nm and the average diameter is 2.94 nm. HRTEM (Fig. 3(c)) imagery shows that N–S-CQD has a lattice spacing of 0.21 nm, which could be attributed to the (100) graphitic crystal phase of graphene [28], suggesting that the synthesized N–S-CQD have a similar core structure to graphite.

The full XPS spectra of N–S-CQD and CQD (Fig. 4 (a)) showed the C 1s (285.1 eV), N 1s (400.1 eV), O 1s (532.1 eV) and S 2p (163.8 eV) peaks of N–S-CQD as well as the C 1s (284.8 eV), N 1s (400.0 eV) and O 1s (532.3 eV) peaks of CQD. The complete XPS spectra show that N–S-CQD and CQD all contain the same three elements (C, O and N), the N element in CQD comes from raw materials, while the presence of the S 2p peak suggests that the N–S-CQD has been successfully doped with S elements. The chemical structure and surface states of N–S-CQD and CQD can be analyzed through high-resolution XPS spectroscopy. In Fig. 4(b) and (f), The high-resolution C 1s spectra of N–S-CQD and CQD have four peaks at C–C/CC (284.5 eV), C–O/C–N (285.5 eV), C–OH (286.1 eV) and CO (287.7 eV) [28–30]. The O 1s spectra of N–S-CQD and CQD in Fig. 4(c) and (g) contain CO/COOH and C–O–C/C–OH peaks located at 531.6 eV and 533.4 eV [28,29], respectively. The N 1s spectra of N–S-CQD and CQD (Fig. 4(d) and (h)) show two peaks at 399.5 eV and 401.0 eV, which correspond to C–N and N–H [30,31]. The S 2p spectra of N–S-CQD (Fig. 4(e)) have peaks at 163.1 eV and 164.3 eV corresponding to the C–S covalent bonding of 2p3/2 and 2p1/2, with the peak at 167.9 eV attributed to SO3 2− 2p3/2 [32].

The FTIR spectra of N–S-CQD and CQD in Fig. 5 (a) show a broad absorption between 3392 and 3409 cm−1 proving the presence of O–H and N–H [33], the peak at 2925 cm−1 is due to C–H [34], the characteristic peaks at 2139 cm−1 and 535 cm−1 correspond to C C and S–S, respectively [35]. The peak at 1595 cm−1 corresponds to CC and CO [36], the N–H, C–N and –COO groups are recognizable as the peaks at 1399 cm−1 [37] and the characteristic peak of C–O is located at 1075 cm−1 [38]. The FTIR analysis are compatible with the XPS results that these chemical bonds and functional groups enhance the hydrophilicity and stability of N–S-CQD in the CL system, suggesting that the heteroatoms through doping enhances the CL properties of N–S-CQD [39].

The XRD spectra of N–S-CQD and CQD were recorded in Fig. 5(b). N–S-CQD and CQD exhibited a diffraction peak with-in the range of 19°–21°, which matches the (002) plane of graphitic carbon [28]. The XRD peaks of N–S-CQD are sharper when compared to CQD, indicating better crystalline properties of N–S-CQD [28].

The Raman patterns of N–S-CQD and CQD are presented in Fig. 5(c). The peaks observed at 1383 cm−1 and 1392 cm−1 correspond to the disordered D bands of N–S-CQD and CQD, suggesting the presence of disordered sp3 carbon [28]. The crystalline G bands of N–S-CQD and CQD are at 1555 cm−1 and 1549 cm−1, respectively [28], which show that N–S-CQD and CQD possess graphitized structures. Notably, the intensity of the G-band surpasses that of the D-band in N–S-CQD and CQD, indicating the prevalence of sp2 structures in the carbon materials. The degree of disorder in carbon materials is assessed using the ID/IG. The ID/IG of N–S-CQD and CQD are 0.75 and 0.84, respectively, indicating that CQD exhibit a higher degree of structural defects and poorer lattice quality [40]and both contain partially amorphous carbon, which is consistent with XRD analyses.

ce:section.ce:section-title: Optical properties and CL stability of N–S-CQD
ce:section.ce:para: Fig. 6 (a) shows that the UV of N–S-CQD shows an absorption peak at 270 nm, which is caused by the π-π* transition of the CC bond and the n-π* transition of the CO bond [41]. In its natural state, N–S-CQD pale yellow and under 365 nm UV light exposure, they exhibit a blue fluorescence (inset of Fig. 6(a)). N–S-CQD exhibit remarkable FL properties with a maximum emission peak occurring at 400 nm when excited at 330 nm. Fig. 6(b) shows that the maximum emission wavelength of N–S-CQD changes from 390 nm to 440 nm when the excitation wavelength goes from 300 nm to 380 nm, which indicates that N–S-CQD has excitation-dependent fluorescence properties [42]. In addition, the fluorescence quantum yields (QY) of the four CQD were determined, the QY of N–S-CQD, S-CQD, N-CQD and CQD were 3.80%, 3.30%, 3.05% and 2.50%, respectively. From the QY results, it is clear that N–S co-doping can better enhance the fluorescence performance.

The stability of N–S-CQD is a critical factor affecting CL signals. Therefore, the short-term stability of N–S-CQD was assessed by leaving it at room temperature for 0, 3, 6, 12, 24 and 48 h, respectively. In addition, N–S-CQD was placed at 4 °C for 30 days, 60 strips and 90 days to examine their long-term placement stability. Fig. 6(c) indicates that the CL strength of N–S-CQD remains almost unchanged during the 48 h of storage, indicating its good short-term stability. The inset of Fig. 6(c) illustrates that N–S-CQD remains clear and transparent after being stored at 4 °C for 30 days (left inset). They remain well-dispersed without agglomeration and the CL intensity shows no significant difference compared to fresh N–S-CQD, indicating that their properties remain unchanged. After 60 days of placement, there was a small decrease in CL signal, but no significant change in appearance (middle inset). When N–S-CQD were stored for 90 days, the CL signals also decreased to a certain extent and a small amount of foam appeared on the surface layer, with some precipitates occurring after standing (right inset), indicating that a certain degree of deterioration may have occurred at this time, but the degree of attenuation of the CL signal was small, which suggests that the long-term stability of N–S-CQD is good.

As a sensitizer for CL systems, N–S-CQD significantly affects the CL signal of SA detection. N–S-CQD was diluted to different concentrations for experiment and the results are depicted in Fig. 7 (a). The highest CL intensities was achieved when N–S-CQD was used as sensitizers at a 50-fold dilution. Therefore, a 50-fold dilution of N–S-CQD was chosen.

KMnO4 exhibits the strongest oxidizing ability in an acidic system that it's important to choose the right concentration of HCl. The concentration of HCHO is set at 6%, while the concentration of KMnO4 remained at 1 mM. The experiments are conducted using HCl concentrations ranging from 0.1 to 1.6 M. Fig. 7(b) shows that the relative CL signal of SA increases significantly with increasing HCl concentration, the relative CL intensity is highest when the HCl concentration reached 0.9 M. Therefore, a concentration of 0.9 M is chosen for HCl.

HCHO can enhance the CL signal in this system, therefore, the concentration of HCHO is optimized to obtain a stronger CL signal. Fig. 7(c) illustrates the CL at various HCHO concentrations. The CL intensity increases with the growing HCHO concentration. When the HCHO concentration is elevated to 11%, the CL signal reaches maximum. Subsequent experimental studies utilize 11% HCHO.

While maintaining constant HCl (0.6 M) and HCHO (6%) concentrations, the sample is tested with varying concentrations of KMnO4. Fig. 7(d) shows that the CL signal is significantly enhanced with increasing KMnO4 concentration. The strongest CL signals were obtained when the concentration of KMnO4 was 1.1 mM for SA detection, so 1.1 mM KMnO4 is choose for the detection of SA.

ce:section.ce:section-title: The linear response range and limit of detection
ce:section.ce:para: The series of standard solutions contained SA are determined using optimized experimental conditions. The results demonstrated that the SA concentration (C) showed a good linear relationship with the relative CL intensity (ΔI) in the range of 50–1600 μM. Fig. 7(e) shows the regression equation for the standard curve is ΔI = 3.08 × 106 C + 1318.30 with an R2 of 0.9952. The relative standard deviation (RSD) of 1.09% is obtained from 11 consecutive determinations of a 20 mM standard SA solution. The limit of detection (LOD) and limit of quantification (LOQ) of this method were calculated to be 0.22 μM and 0.73 μM, respectively (LOD = 3 SD/K, LOQ = 10 SD/K, where SD is the standard deviation and K is the slope of the linear regression equation).

The kinetic curve of CL in Fig. 7(f) indicates that the KMnO4 system exhibits low CL intensity. However, the new KMnO4-(N–S-CQD) system with the addition of N–S-CQD exhibiting a strong CL signal, its observation suggests that N–S-CQD has a significant sensitizing effect on the system. When SA is added, the CL intensity was significantly reduced, indicating that SA can suppress the CL signal of this system. From the CL kinetic curves, it is evident that the reaction is very rapid and transient.

The mechanism of the CL reaction was investigated by UV analysis of the system and samples. The characteristic absorption peaks of SA and KMnO4 are at 410 nm and 540 nm, respectively. When the SA is mixed with KMnO4, the characteristic absorption peak at 540 nm is significantly reduced, it indicates a redox reaction between SA and KMnO4. Additionally, a new absorption peak appeared at 300 nm, indicate the formation of a new intermediate. When SA, HCHO and N–S-CQD exist simultaneously, the general trend of the UV absorption curves remained unchanged. However, the intensity of the absorption peak at 540 nm was significantly reduced, indicating that HCHO and N–S-CQD can significantly enhance the CL reaction [38].

Based on the above analysis, the possible CL mechanism is postulated, as shown in Fig. 8 . KMnO4 undergoes a redox reaction with SA in an HCl medium and releases energy, causing the reactants disappear and the corresponding oxidation products [43]. When SA is directly oxidized, KMnO4 interacts with SA and forms the excited state Mn (II-IV) *. When the excited state Mn (II-IV) * returns to the ground state Mn (II-IV), it releases unstable relaxation energy, resulting in the production of an optical signal and unstable intermediates. In the course of the reaction, HCl provides the acidic conditions for the reaction system, HCHO enhances the intensity of CL because it reacts directly with the oxidant, thereby accelerating the oxidation rate [44]. The functional groups NH, –OH and –COOH give N–S-CQD good water solubility, this property enables them to act as effective penetrating agents in the KMnO4 system, thereby establishing the new KMnO4-(N–S-CQD) system. In the established new system, N–S-CQD can act as catalysts for the CL reaction promoting the generation of free radicals and enhancing CL signals. Meanwhile, the strong oxidizing ability of N–S-CQD can accelerate the redox reaction, thereby significantly increase the CL signal of the system [45]. There are two reasons why SA can inhibit the CL intensity of the system. Firstly, SA will compete with N–S-CQD to consume KMnO4 when it is oxidized by KMnO4, resulting in a decrease in CL signal. Secondly, SA affected the degree of activation of N–S-CQD and weakened its catalytic effect, which also affects the CL intensity [46]. In this process, the system exhibits a higher CL signal, enhancing the ability for signal amplification (SA) detection and broadening the range of SA detection.

Under optimized experimental conditions, interference was assessed by analyzing a standard SA solution (0.3 mM) to which various potentially interfering substances were added. The results in Table 1 demonstrated the feasibility of the method for detecting SA in actual samples.

Add 10 mL ethyl acetate to 10 g pork, chicken and fish samples, then extract for 5 min, respectively. Subsequently, add 2 mL HCl (0.1 M) to the extract and rotate the evaporated mixture to reduce the volume to approximately 2 mL. The remaining solution is mixed with 1 mL of hydrochloric acid and 2 mL of hexane, vertexing vigorously for 30 s and then centrifuging for 5 min (12,000 r/min), the retained lower solution was filtered through a 0.22 μm membrane to obtain a sample solution for further analysis [47]. The SA standard solution was added to the prepared pork, chicken and fish samples at a concentration of 0.2 mM and the results ranged from 0.20 to 0.22 mM, which provided preliminary evidence that the three biomass samples were free of SA residues. Different concentrations of standard SA were added to each of the test samples and the recoveries were assessed. Table 2 shows that the recoveries ranged from 97.5% to 105.0%, proving that the method can be used for the determination of SA in real samples.

The results of different methods for the determination of SA are presented in Table 3 . The new method developed in this study exhibits a border linear range and a lower detection limit compared to other reported detection methods.

N–S-CQD synthesized by hydrothermal method using Artemisia annua L. as raw material and dl-cysteine as co-dopant exhibit several advantageous properties for sensing applications, including uniform particle size, stability, high water solubility and excellent CL characteristics. N–S-CQD can be acted as a nano-catalyst in the KMnO4 system, the new KMnO4-(N–S-CQD) system that has been developed can be used as an effective and sensitive CL sensor for the detection of SA concentrations, which has been applied to the determination of SA in pork, chicken and fish samples with satisfactory results. Additionally, we stated the possible CL mechanism for KMnO4-(N–S-CQD)-SA. The proposed detection method has advantages such as ease of operation, high sensitivity, cost-effectiveness and robust anti-interference capabilities, rendering it fitting for the determination of SA residues in real samples. The new developed method expands the applicability of CQD and provides valuable reference data for the analysis of veterinary drug residues in food.
The authors declare no competing financial interest.

Yue Zhao: Writing – review & editing, Writing – original draft, Validation, Investigation, Data curation. Xian Yang: Resources, Project administration, Funding acquisition. Hongping Han: Supervision. Luoyilan Zhang: Investigation. Ke Cheng: Investigation. Yujie Xiao: Writing – review & editing. Xuan Tan: Formal analysis. Hao Xue: Formal analysis. Xue Zhang: Software, Formal analysis. Hong Zheng: Writing – review & editing, Resources, Methodology, Funding acquisition.

ce:conflict-of-interest.ce:para: The authors declare that they have no known competing financial interests or personal relationships that could have appeared to influence the work reported in this paper.

ce:acknowledgment.ce:para: We gratefully acknowledge the Science and Technology Research Program of the Chongqing Municipal Education Commission (No. KJZD-K202300504).

sb:title.sb:maintitle: Molecularly imprinted polyresorcinol based capacitive sensor for sulphanilamide detection

ce:bib-reference.ce:source-text: Prusty AK, Bhand S. Molecularly Imprinted Polyresorcinol Based Capacitive Sensor for Sulphanilamide Detection. Electroanalysis. 2019;31(9):1797-1808.

sb:title.sb:maintitle: Modifications of Au nanoparticle-functionalized graphene for sensitive detection of sulfanilamide
